ontology.foodex2_description: Ice lollies and similar frozen confections
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for popsicle / ice pop as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for frozen confections is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard popsicle on a stick weighs approximately 75 g, which is a typical single-serve portion sold commercially in Singapore and internationally.

nutrient_source.title: Popsicle (ice pop, fruit-flavoured) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Frozen novelties, ice type, popsicle (NDB 19270)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Frozen novelties, ice type, popsicle' (NDB 19270 / FDC ID 167582). Both sources are consistent for energy, carbohydrate, sugar, and sodium. Saturated fat reported as 0.0 g/100g in USDA SR Legacy for this product class. Detailed fatty acid breakdown, potassium, and several micronutrients not reported in HPB; USDA SR Legacy values used to supplement where available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for ice confection / sugar-water frozen dessert
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry for a generic fruit-flavoured popsicle exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous entry: sucrose-sweetened ice confection / sugar-water frozen dessert. The food is essentially a frozen sucrose-water solution (17.1 g sugar/100g, 0 g fibre, 0.3 g protein, 0.1 g fat) with no glycaemic buffering. Sucrose GI is approximately 65 per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycaemic load per 75 g serving = (65 × 17.1 × 0.75) / 100 = 8.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (17.1 g/100g) with zero dietary fibre promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and endothelial dysfunction, independently elevating blood pressure risk despite negligible sodium (6 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit popsicle consumption to occasional small portions and prefer frozen whole-fruit alternatives (e.g., frozen mango slices, frozen berries) that provide fibre and lower free-sugar density, thereby reducing the insulin-driven sodium-retention pathway.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 17.1 g/100g with dietary fibre at 0 g/100g and GI ~65 produces a rapid postprandial blood glucose spike with no glycaemic buffering; a 75 g serving delivers approximately 12.8 g free sugar and a glycaemic load of 8.3.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid standard popsicles; if a frozen treat is desired, choose sugar-free or no-added-sugar variants sweetened with non-nutritive sweeteners, or opt for frozen whole-fruit pieces that provide fibre to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar load (17.1 g/100g, all sugar, zero fibre) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia, hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and elevated LDL particle count when consumed regularly, increasing cardiovascular risk independent of fat content.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat popsicles as an occasional indulgence only, keeping total free-sugar intake within WHO guidelines (less than 10% of total energy), and favour frozen fruit options that provide antioxidants and fibre with lower sugar density.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 17.1 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g refined-sugar threshold associated with pro-inflammatory mediator upregulation; no anti-inflammatory factors (omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants, fibre) are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular popsicle consumption; choose frozen berry-based or homemade unsweetened fruit-puree popsicles that provide polyphenols and antioxidants with substantially lower free-sugar content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A popsicle (also called an ice lolly, ice pop, or freeze pop) is a frozen confection made from sweetened fruit-flavoured water or juice frozen around a flat wooden or plastic stick. The product is essentially a frozen sugar-water solution: per 100 g it provides 67 kcal, 17.1 g carbohydrate (all sugar), 0.3 g protein, 0.1 g fat, 0 g dietary fibre, and only 6 mg sodium. The texture is firm and icy when frozen, melting rapidly at room temperature to a thin, sweet liquid. Flavour is typically fruit-forward — strawberry, orange, grape, or tropical variants — derived from natural or artificial flavouring rather than whole fruit, which explains the absence of fibre and micronutrient density. Popsicles are widely consumed as a refreshing snack in hot climates including Singapore, and are popular with children. Clinically, the high free-sugar content (17.1 g/100g) with zero fibre and a GI of approximately 65 makes this a high-concern food for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, as a single 75 g stick delivers ~12.8 g free sugar and a glycaemic load of 8.3. The sugar load also raises moderate concern for hypertension via insulin-mediated sodium retention and for cardiovascular disease via hypertriglyceridaemia pathways. The food is vegan, dairy-free, gluten-free, and low in sodium, making it suitable for those dietary restrictions, but the high sugar content warrants a clear warning label for metabolic health conditions.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in a popsicle?
Can a diabetic eat a popsicle?
How many calories in a popsicle?
What is the glycemic index of a popsicle?
Is ice lolly the same as popsicle?
Can I eat popsicle if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for frozen novelties, ice type, popsicle (NDB 19270). Both sources are consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed source, which is the best available approved evidence for this food class.
notes: Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol are reported as 0.0 g/100g: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 19270 for this frozen ice-type confection with negligible fat (total fat 0.1 g/100g). Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100g: confirmed zero per HPB and USDA SR Legacy — this product contains no whole fruit or fibre-contributing ingredients. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed vitamin and trace mineral data for generic fruit-flavoured popsicle not individually reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy NDB 19270 as of 2026. Potassium reported as 3 mg/100g per USDA SR Legacy NDB 19270. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose-sweetened ice confection from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no exact popsicle GI entry exists in the approved GI database. Serving size of 75 g is based on a typical commercially available single-stick popsicle portion. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. The food is classified as a generic international product.
